title: NOC 53123 – Theatre, Fashion, Exhibit and Other Creative Designers | GTR Immigration
description: Canada’s creative economy thrives on the innovation of designers categorized under NOC 53123. From the bustling fashion runways of Montreal to the world-class theatre stages of Stratford and the massive exhibit halls in Toronto, these TEER 3 professionals are essential to Canada’s cultural and commercial identity. For immigration, this category is particularly attractive for the Self-Employed Persons Program and specialized PNP draws targeting the creative sector.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category5 – Occupations in art, culture, recreation and sportTEER Level3Major Group53 – Occupations in art, culture and sportSub-Major Group531 – Occupations in art and cultureMinor Group5312 – Occupations in creative and performing artsNOC Code53123

duties: Conceptualize and create designs for clothing, theatrical productions, or museum exhibits.
Select materials, colors, and themes for specific design projects.
Supervise the technical production of designs, including tailoring or construction.
Direct the preparation of sketches, models, and computer-aided designs (CAD).
Coordinate with clients, directors, and museum curators to meet project specifications.
employment_requirements: A university degree or college diploma in fine arts or a specialized design field is usually required.
Creative ability and talent, demonstrated by a portfolio of work, are essential.
Several years of experience in a related field may be required for senior positions.
job_outlook: The outlook is Stable to Favourable. Over the next decade, an estimated 7,600 new job openings will arise from both expansion and replacement needs. Digital literacy (CAD/3D modeling) will be a significant factor in employability.
labour_market_demand: Demand is consistently high in urban centers like Toronto, Montreal, and Vancouver. The growth of the "experience economy" has spiked the need for exhibit designers in museums and corporate events, while Canada’s film industry ("Hollywood North") maintains a steady requirement for costume and set designers.
Global Recognition: Canada hosts major events like the Toronto International Film Festival (TIFF) and Montreal Fashion Week.
Supportive Grants: Access to federal and provincial arts council funding.
Creative Hubs: Work in dedicated districts known for artistic innovation and collaboration.
pathways: Self-Employed Persons Program: For world-class designers with significant independent experience.
Express Entry (FSW/CEC): Valid for those with corporate or institutional employment history.
OINP Employer Job Offer: Targeting professionals in the fashion and film/theatre sectors.
